1: vayomer Bil'am el Balaq bane li vezeh shiv'ah mizbechot vahakon li vezeh shiv'ah farim veshiv'ah eilim: Balaam commands seven altars, seven bulls, seven rams — a sacral number prominent in ancient sacrificial contexts across cultures (Ugaritic texts mention seven-fold sacrificial preparations). The number seven signals sacred completeness. Notably, Balaam directs the construction and sacrifice — he is functioning as a cultic officiant despite being a Gentile diviner, and the text presents this without critique.
2: Balak complies exactly with Balaam's prescription. The narrative emphasizes Balak's obedience to Balaam's ritual instructions throughout — an ironic reversal of the expected patron-client relationship. The king becomes the ritual assistant to the diviner he hired to serve him.
3: vayomer Bil'am leBaltq hityatsev al olatkha ve'elchah ulay yiqreh YHWH liqrati: Balaam instructs Balak to 'stand by your burnt offering while I go.' The verb yiqreh (will happen/meet) from qarah is deliberately vague — 'perhaps something will happen to me' — suggesting Balaam is not certain of the mode of divine communication he will receive. He will 'report what YHWH shows me' (mah yar'eni). The uncertainty of the encounter preparation is authentic to prophetic experience: the prophet positions himself for encounter but cannot control its occurrence.
4: vayiqar Elohim el Bil'am vayomer elav et shiv'at hamizbeachot arakhti: God meets Balaam and Balaam reports his sacrificial preparations. The verb vayiqar (met/encountered) is from qarah (to happen upon, to meet by chance or in encounter) — the same root Balaam used in v3 when he hoped God would 'happen' to him. The encounter formula underscores divine initiative: God comes to Balaam, not vice versa. Balaam's report of his sacrifice preparations is a form of presenting his prophetic credentials to the deity.
5: vayasem YHWH davar beffi Bil'am: 'The LORD put a word in Balaam's mouth.' The word-in-mouth idiom for prophetic inspiration appears in its clearest form here — the same verb yasim (put/place) Balaam used in Numbers 22:38. This establishes that the first oracle (Numbers 23:7-10) is entirely YHWH's composition, not Balaam's craft. Whatever Balaam's intentions or background, the words delivered are divine words placed in a human mouth.
6: Balak stands by his burnt offering with all the Moabite princes as Balaam returns — the elaborate ritual setting up of sacrifice was preparation for this moment of prophetic address. The princes as audience make the oracle a public state event, not a private consultation.
7: vayissa meshalow vayomar min Aram yancheni Balaq: 'He took up (yissa, lifted up) his oracle (mashal) and said.' The term mashal is translated as oracle/parable/proverb — it is the genre label for Balaam's four speeches. The root mashal means 'to be like/comparable to' (the comparative function) and also 'to rule over/govern' — both semantic fields are active in prophetic meshalim. The opening of the first oracle identifies the setting: Aram (northern Mesopotamia) and the mountains of the east — Balaam's homeland, from which he was summoned by Moab.
8: mah eqov lo qabboh El umah ez'om lo za'am YHWH: 'How can I curse whom God has not cursed? How can I denounce whom YHWH has not denounced?' Three curse roots appear across the oracles: arar (structural curse), qabav (to wound/bore-curse), and za'am (to denounce/be furious at). The rhetorical questions are unanswerable — the entire metaphysics of ancient cursing depends on the deity endorsing the curse; without divine sanction, the words have no power. Balaam does not merely refuse to curse — he states the ontological impossibility of cursing what God has blessed.
9: ki merosh tsurim er'enu umigeva'ot ashurennu hen am levadad yishkon uvagoyim lo yithchashav: 'From the rocky peaks I see them... here is a people who dwell alone (levadad yishkon) and do not count themselves among the nations.' The phrase am levadad (a people of aloneness) is Israel's essential character — set apart, not assimilated into the nations' categories. Later Jewish interpretation (Midrash Bamidbar Rabbah) reads this as divine comfort: Israel's distinctiveness is the source of their preservation. Balaam perceives from Baal's high place what Baal cannot provide: the theological truth about Israel's unique status.
10: mi manah afar Ya'aqov umispar et roba' Yisra'el: 'Who can count the dust of Jacob or number the fourth part of Israel?' The uncountable dust is the Abrahamic promise language (Genesis 13:16: 'if anyone can count the dust of the earth, so your offspring can be counted'). Balaam blesses Israel with the terms of the Abrahamic covenant without knowing the covenant explicitly — YHWH has placed this blessing in his mouth. The oracle closes with the personal wish: 'May I die the death of the upright (yesharim, righteous/straight ones), may my end be like theirs' — Balaam desires Israel's destiny even as he serves Moab's enemy purposes.
11: vayomer Balaq el Bil'am meh asita li laqov oyvai laqachticha vahineh berakhta varakh: Balak's shocked response: 'What have you done to me? I took you to curse my enemies and you have blessed them — blessed (berakhta varakh)!' The doubled blessing idiom emphasizes the completeness of what Balaam delivered. Balak's frustration is understandable from his perspective, but the narrative invites the reader to recognize that the blessing is exactly right — Balaam was commissioned by YHWH, not Balak.
12: vayaan vayomer halo et asher yasim YHWH beffi oto eshmor ledabber: 'Must I not be careful to say what YHWH puts in my mouth?' Balaam's defense is the same prophetic constraint of Numbers 22:38 — he simply delivers what is placed in his mouth. The word eshmor (I must guard/keep) suggests active fidelity to the received word, not passive transmission. The prophet guards the word as a custodian of divine speech.
13: Balak proposes a new vantage point — only the edge of the camp, not the full mass. His theory is that a partial view of Israel (rather than the overwhelming totality) might enable a partial curse. The change of perspective strategy is magical thinking: that changing the angle of vision changes the divine verdict. The narrative will refute this in the second oracle.
14: vayiqachehu sadeh Tsofim el rosh haPisgah: Pisgah — the same mountain from whose summit Moses will later see the promised land before his death (Deuteronomy 34:1). The geographic resonance is significant: the place where Balaam speaks oracles of blessing becomes the place where Moses receives his final vision of the covenant's fulfillment. Seven new altars, seven new animals — the full ritual repeated.
15: Balaam instructs Balak to stand by his offering while he goes to meet YHWH. The second oracle setup mirrors the first — the repetition establishes that Balaam's prophetic procedure is consistent and the outcome will be YHWH's, not varied by Balak's strategy of changing locations.
16: vayiqar YHWH el Bil'am vayasem davar beffi vayomer shuv el Balaq vekhoh tedabber: YHWH meets Balaam and places words in his mouth — the exact formula of v5. The identity of the divine speaker shifts between YHWH and Elohim across the chapter, but the action is identical. The words placed in Balaam's mouth are again entirely YHWH's composition.
17: Balak is standing by his burnt offering — the patient posture of waiting for prophetic word. His question 'What has YHWH said?' (mah dibber YHWH) acknowledges YHWH as the source of Balaam's oracle — a remarkable concession from a Moabite king. The story has effectively educated Balak about who controls the prophetic word even if he cannot accept the content of the oracles.
18: vayissa meshalow vayomar qum Balaq ushma hazinah adai ben Tzippor: The second oracle begins with a call to attention: 'Arise, Balak, and hear! Give ear to me, son of Zippor!' (hazinah, from azan, to give ear/listen attentively). This is more direct and authoritative than the first oracle's scenic opening — Balaam addresses Balak personally. The imperatives escalate the prophetic authority.
19: lo ish El viykhazev uven adam veyitneham: 'God is not a man who lies, nor a son of man who changes his mind.' The verse is one of the deepest theological statements in Numbers — divine immutability articulated by a Gentile prophet. The contrast ish...ben adam (man...son of man) establishes the human-divine distinction: humans lie (kazav) and repent/change their minds (nicham). The question 'Does he speak and not act?' is answered by the oracles themselves — YHWH spoke blessing over Israel, and it will not be reversed. This verse becomes a key text for divine aseity and reliability (cf. 1 Samuel 15:29, which echoes it verbatim in a different context).
20: hineh varech laqachti uvarakh velo ashivenah: 'I have received a command to bless; he has blessed, and I cannot reverse it.' The verb laqachti (I have received/taken) describes Balaam as the recipient of a blessing already given — he did not compose it or have authority over it. The blessing is YHWH's prior decision (varakh velo ashivenah, he blessed and I cannot reverse it). The prophet cannot revoke what the deity has determined — prophetic speech is declarative of divine reality, not creative of it.
21: lo hibit aven beYa'aqov velo ra'ah amal beYisra'el YHWH Elohav immo: 'He has not looked upon (hibit) iniquity in Jacob nor has he seen (ra'ah) trouble in Israel.' This verse is theologically contested: does it mean (a) YHWH perceives no sin in Israel (impossible given the wilderness narrative), or (b) YHWH does not look on Israel as accursed/condemned (i.e., no curse-worker can penetrate the divine favor), or (c) no divination-magic (aven can mean both wickedness and divination) has been found working against Israel. The context favors (b) or (c): in a chapter about Balaam's inability to curse, the claim is that no magical-cursing power (aven, amal) is effective against Israel because YHWH is with them. The clause YHWH Elohav immo (YHWH his God is with him) is the protective reality that nullifies curse-attempts.
22: El motzi'am miMitsrayim ketho'afot re'em lo: 'God brought them out of Egypt — like the horns of a wild ox (re'em) for him.' The re'em is the wild ox/aurochs (Bos primigenius, now extinct) — the most powerful bovine creature known in the ancient world. Its horns (to'afot, the exaltation/horns) represent irresistible power. The Exodus is framed as a display of divine wild-ox power — uncageable, unstoppable strength that liberated Israel. The LXX reads monokeros (one-horned), which entered the Latin Vulgate as unicornis — hence KJV's 'unicorn' in this verse.
23: ki lo nachash beYa'aqov velo qesem beYisra'el: 'There is no omen (nachash, from nachash, to divine/observe omens — the same root as 'serpent'!) that works against Jacob, no divination (qesem) that prevails against Israel.' The irony is profound: Balaam's entire expertise is qesem (divination), and he declares that divination cannot work against Israel. He invalidates his own professional tool in the course of the oracle. The following clause — 'at the right time it will be said of Jacob and Israel what God has done' — replaces divination with proclamation: instead of oracles determining Israel's fate, their fate will be declared after the fact as testimony to what God did.
24: hen am kelavi yaqum uchlabbi yitnas': 'The people rise like a lioness (lavi), they rouse themselves like a lion (ari).' The lion metaphors for Israel appear again in Numbers 24:9 — Israel as a lion that does not rest until it has devoured its prey. Jacob's blessing uses the lion metaphor for Judah (Genesis 49:9), and Balaam's oracle develops this national lion image. The military connotation is clear: Israel awakens like a lion to prey, drinks the blood of the slain — victor language.
25: Balak's desperate compromise: 'Neither curse them nor bless them!' — the third option when the first two have both produced the wrong result. But this is impossible: YHWH's prophetic word cannot simply be suspended. The oracle must be delivered; neutral silence is not an option when the divine word has been placed in the prophet's mouth.
26: vayaan Bil'am vayomer el Balaq halo dibbarti elekha lemor kol asher yedabber YHWH oto e'eseh: Balaam's refrain — 'Did I not tell you from the beginning that I must do everything YHWH says?' — establishes prophetic consistency. The word e'eseh (I shall do) is active: the prophet actively performs YHWH's commission, not passively reports it.
27: Balak proposes a third location: 'Maybe it will please God (yiytar beeinei haElohim, it will be good/right in God's eyes) for you to curse them from there.' The persistence is notable — Balak still believes the location is the variable. He has not understood that the problem is not the vantage point but YHWH's sovereign determination to bless.
28: vayiqach Balaq et Bil'am rosh haPe'or hashaqif al penei hayeshimon: Peor overlooks hayeshimon (the wasteland/desert) rather than the Israelite camp — the third vantage point gives a less direct view than the previous two. This location will become infamous as the site of the Baal Peor incident (Numbers 25), where Israel itself commits apostasy. The same mountain from which Balaam can only bless Israel will soon be the site of Israel's worst wilderness sin.
29: Balaam's third oracle preparation: seven altars again, seven bulls and seven rams. The repetition of the full ritual three times has the effect of underlining that no variation in ritual preparation changes the divine outcome. The ritual theater is identical each time; only the divine word changes.
30: Balak's exact compliance: 'Balak did as Balaam directed and offered a bull and a ram on each altar.' The report is brief and formulaic — by the third time, the reader understands the pattern. The ritual preparation is complete; the oracle will now break the pattern of the first two.
